So, you were on Amazon for a few years, two and a half years or so.
So, what, what, how has that affected how you run your company?
Is it, you know, like, do you use the two pizza rule, you know, what's the things that you take from Amazon that happens right now inside of Convoy?
Team structure is part of it, but I don't know if that was just because of, you know, the experience at Amazon.
One thing that Amazon, I think, does really well is they have a set of company values that they, that they live day in, day out.
So, you know, I had different experience in each of these companies, so that could have impacted this as well.
But I worked at Microsoft and I worked at Google as well.
And in neither case did I feel like the company's values were sort of lived day to day in everything we did.

Like, I'm pulling from this value and I'm challenging on this based on the company value.
So we're, what we, we did, um, actually asked, so Jeff Bezos is one of our investors and I kind of asked him how, when they created the company values.

Um, and so we also, we waited a little while, kind of towards about the year mark, we started working on our values.

So I think values is a huge part of what I took from Amazon.
The second one was just transparency.
One thing Amazon does that I think is really smart is, you know, there's time for this to work and time where maybe it wouldn't work is they don't do PowerPoints, right?
So you walk in, it's the six-pager, you write a document, you walk in, it reads it in silence before the meeting starts.
And then you discuss it after that.
So you might walk into a one-hour meeting and talk and read for 30 minutes and everybody marks it up and then you have a conversation for the next 30 minutes.

So it's not like the CEO has 20 goals that he cares about.

And the Word document also is really about a transparency and accountability because you can't mask things in a Word document.

So really create, at least from my experience, was this sort of open, transparent culture where it was based on merit and what you did, much harder to mask.

I was like, wow, that's impressive to me that they've developed these things that allow you to have this kind of straightforward discussion.

So each individual team feels like, I have a lot of responsibility to do this because, you know, the SVPs or the CEOs are going to see this goal.

And so these seven goals were rolling to this goal.
And then at that level, the other four goals were rolling to this goal.
And then the CEO would have like 15 or 20 goals.
And so there was kind of a watered-down effect of like, yeah, but my goal rolls into this bigger goal.
So the guy doesn't really know.
Just it felt different, I guess, in terms of day-to-day operations.

but you've learned it and then actually applied it to your company?

So, but think about reviews at Amazon, right?
The reviews team at Amazon originally created, they were solving the problem of how do I have confidence, how do I give my customers confidence to buy?
So the problem was customer confidence or I can't talk to a sales rep or I can't touch and feel this thing before I buy it.
So how do I get past that?
Well, customer confidence comes from validation via other customers, social proof, right?
And that was the problem they were solving.
And then they came up with the reviews as the solution.

And then everyone they ever hired from there on out was hired to the reviews team.
